name: Capture API callbacks with Airtable hands-free

parameters.text: =✅ Capture API callbacks with Airtable hands-free — run completed at {{ $now.toFormat('HH:mm') }}

parameters.content:
### Receive Inbound Webhook

Entry point: listens for POST calls on `/wf10327-record`. Use the TEST url while building, switch callers to the PRODUCTION url after publishing.

Next: Map to Canonical Schema

### Map to Canonical Schema

Shapes the item: writes `recordId`, `kind`, `payload`. Keeps the schema explicit between stages.

Next: Route by Event Kind

### Route by Event Kind

Routes each item to one of 3 lanes: created, updated + fallback.

Ports: ①→ Handle 'created' → Airtable · ②→ Handle 'updated' → Forward API · ③→ Log Unrecognized Kind

### Handle 'created' → Airtable

Upserts into Airtable's 'Records' table, matched on `recordId` — safe to re-run without creating duplicates.

Ports: ①→ Consolidate Handling · ②→ Capture Failure Context

↻ retries 3×/2s · ⚠ failures exit via the error rail · 🔑 Airtable PAT

### Handle 'updated' → Forward API

POST https://api.example.com/v1/records — retries 3× with backoff on failure.

Ports: ①→ Consolidate Handling · ②→ Capture Failure Context

↻ retries 3×/2s · ⚠ failures exit via the error rail · 🔑 API key / bearer for the target API

### Log Unrecognized Kind

Creates a new record in Airtable's 'Records' table.

Ports: ①→ Consolidate Handling · ②→ Capture Failure Context

↻ retries 3×/2s · ⚠ failures exit via the error rail · 🔑 Airtable PAT

### Consolidate Handling

Rejoins 3 branches into one stream (mode: append) so downstream logic runs once.

Next: Respond with Outcome

### Respond with Outcome

Sends the HTTP 200 response back to the caller — keep this fast; heavy work happens after/async.

Next: Save Execution Metadata

### Save Execution Metadata

Saves key fields onto the execution record — searchable later in n8n's execution list.

Next: Tag Pipeline Metadata

### Tag Pipeline Metadata

Shapes the item: writes `pipeline`, `version`. Keeps the schema explicit between stages.

Next: Stamp Processing Time

### Stamp Processing Time

Stamps `processedAt` with the current time for auditing.

Next: Send Summary to Slack

### Send Summary to Slack

Posts a message to Slack (`#data-ops`) — a low-latency way for the team to see automation activity without checking a dashboard.

↻ retries 3×/2s · ⚠ failures exit via the error rail · 🔑 Slack OAuth2

### Capture Failure Context

Shapes the item: writes `failedNode`, `errorMessage`, `workflow`. Keeps the schema explicit between stages.

Next: Escalate Failure to Slack

### Escalate Failure to Slack

Posts a message to Slack (`#data-ops`) — a low-latency way for the team to see automation activity without checking a dashboard.

↻ retries 3×/2s · 🔑 Slack OAuth2

# Capture API callbacks with Airtable hands-free

**Pattern:** Webhook Pipeline · **Level:** Core · **Trigger:** Webhook

## What it does
Capture API callbacks with Airtable hands-free. It fires the moment an external system calls its webhook.

## Why it exists
Manual migration is slow and error-prone; this workflow makes the process repeatable, monitored and hands-off, with failures surfaced instead of silently lost.

## Main flow
Receive Inbound Webhook → Map to Canonical Schema → Route by Event Kind → Handle 'created' → Airtable → Consolidate Handling → Respond with Outcome → Save Execution Metadata → …

## Use it for
- Teams that want to webhook pipeline without manual effort
- Anyone automating migration in data
- Replacing a fragile manual process with a reliable workflow

## You get
- Keeps your systems in sync
- Saves hours of manual work each week
- Removes copy-paste errors

## Before first run
Connect credentials: API key / bearer for the target API; Airtable PAT; Slack OAuth2.
Follow the color key: 🟦 entry · 🟪 AI · 🟫 actions · ⬜ utilities · 🟩 outputs · 🟥 error rail.
Each node has its own doc card underneath — read those before editing.

## Try it now
Sample data is pinned on the trigger — hit **Execute Workflow** to watch it flow through every node before connecting any real credentials. Unpin the trigger (right-click → Unpin) when you're ready to go live.

## Design decisions

The optional HMAC check runs before any business logic touches the payload — bad signatures are rejected at the door rather than after work has already been done with untrusted data. Acking fast (HTTP 202) and doing the real work after the response is what stops the sender's webhook delivery from timing out and re-delivering the same event.

## Practice exercises

Once it's wired up with real credentials, try:

1. Turn on signature verification and trace what a tampered payload does at the 'Signature Valid?' gate.
2. Add a new event kind to the switch and give it its own handler.
3. Remove the fast-ack step and observe how response time changes under a slow downstream write.

## 🛟 Error handling & retries

External calls retry **3× with 2s backoff**. Anything still failing exits through its node's error output into the red error rail → context is captured and the on-call channel is alerted. Set an n8n **Error Workflow** in settings as the final safety net.

## 🔐 Security

Never trust inbound payloads: the webhook path is unguessable. Add header auth or IP allow-listing for defense in depth. Secrets live in n8n credentials — never in node parameters.

## 🛠 Customize me

Quick wins: point the data nodes at your own accounts (see 🔑 lines on each card), adjust the trigger cadence, and tune thresholds/labels to your migration process. Every node card explains its role — safe to extend at any marked branch.
